decision_summary: The chip temperature (89.26°C) exceeds the limit (85.0°C) by ~4.26°C. Previous iterations likely exhausted simple material increases or hit diminishing returns. Following the strategy note to use coordinated multi-variable changes, I will simultaneously increase the heat spreader's conductivity and its width. This combines better lateral conduction with a larger surface area to dissipate heat towards the side boundaries.

changes.reason: Increasing conductivity beyond the nominal 500 W/(m*K) (simulating a higher-grade composite or metal matrix) improves lateral heat spreading efficiency, reducing the thermal gradient from the chip center to the edges.
Widening the spreader increases the cross-sectional area available for heat flow towards the Dirichlet boundaries (left/right walls at 25°C), directly lowering thermal resistance.

expected_effects: More uniform temperature distribution across the spreader
Increased heat flux towards the left and right domain boundaries
risk_notes: Widening the spreader to 1.05m approaches the domain width (1.2m); ensure it does not interfere with boundary condition application zones if they are edge-exclusive.
Conductivity increase assumes availability of advanced materials; if this is a strict physical constraint, geometry changes alone must suffice in future steps.
Coordinated changes may overshoot the target if both variables have high sensitivity; monitor margin closely.
